dublinCore.dc:title: 119 HR 9329 IH: SEC Reform and Restructuring Act
dublinCore.dc:rights: Pursuant to Title 17 Section 105 of the United States Code, this file is not subject to copyright protection and is in the public domain.

action.action-desc: Mrs. Wagner (for herself, Mr. Downing, Mr. Sessions, and Mr. Huizenga) introduced the following bill; which was referred to the Committee on Financial Services

form.official-title: To make improvements to the securities laws, and for other purposes.

subsection.text: This Act may be cited as the SEC Reform and Restructuring Act.
The table of contents for this Act is as follows:

toc.toc-entry: Sec. 1. Short title; table of contents.
Sec. 101. Consideration by the Securities and Exchange Commission of the costs and benefits of regulations and certain other agency actions of the Commission.
Sec. 201. Semiannual testimony to Congress regarding activities of the Securities and Exchange Commission.
Sec. 301. GAO audit of information technology infrastructure and handling of data.
Title IV—Review the Expansion of Government
Sec. 401. Consideration of cumulative effect of regulations required.
Title V—Streamlining Public Company Accounting Oversight
Sec. 501. Transfer of Public Company Accounting Oversight Board to Securities and Exchange Commission.
Sec. 504. Auditing, quality control, standards, and rules.
Sec. 509. Rule of construction with respect to cooperative arrangements.
Title VI—Study Regarding Major Rules Issued by the Securities and Exchange Commission
Sec. 601. GAO study regarding major rules.
Sec. 801. Determination of the number of violations.

section.header: Consideration by the Securities and Exchange Commission of the costs and benefits of regulations and certain other agency actions of the Commission
section.text: Section 23 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78w) is amended by adding at the end the following:

paragraph.text: Before proposing a regulation, the Commission shall—

subparagraph.text: clearly identify the nature and source of the problem that the regulation is designed to address, as well as assess the significance of that problem, to enable assessment of whether any new regulation is warranted; and
ensure that the regulation would be within the Commission’s jurisdiction and that the Commission has sufficient experience and expertise to regulate the subject matter covered by the regulation.

paragraph.header: Requirements for issuing a proposed or final regulation

subparagraph.text: In issuing a proposed or final regulation, the Commission shall—

clause.text: clearly identify the market participants who will be impacted by the regulation;

utilize the Chief Economist of the Commission to assess the costs and benefits, both qualitative and quantitative, of the regulation, both on the regulation’s own and cumulatively with other existing and related proposed regulations;
only issue the regulation if the Commission makes a reasoned determination that the benefits of the regulation justify the costs of the regulation;
ensure that the regulation is accessible, consistent, written in plain language, and easy to understand; and
ensure that the length of the public comment period is commensurate with the complexity of the regulation and the expected public interest in the rulemaking.

subparagraph.header: Inclusion of information in a proposed or final regulation
subparagraph.text: In issuing a proposed or final regulation, the Commission shall include in the regulation—

clause.text: the results of the identifications and assessments required under clauses (i) and (ii) of subparagraph (A) with respect to the regulation;
an explanation of why the regulation meets the regulatory objectives of the Commission more effectively than other available alternatives;
a description of how the Commission intends the regulation to interact with existing regulations and proposed regulations; and
a justification of the length of the public comment period for the regulation.

subparagraph.text: In deciding whether and how to regulate, the Commission shall assess the costs and benefits of available regulatory alternatives, including the alternative of not regulating, and choose the approach that maximizes benefits net of costs, to the extent quantifiable. Specifically, the Commission shall—

clause.text: consistent with the requirements of section 3(f) (15 U.S.C. 78c(f)), section 2(b) of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. 77b(b)), section 202(c) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80b–2(c)), and section 2(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80a–2(c)), consider whether a rulemaking (both on the regulation’s own and cumulatively with other existing and proposed regulations), in addition to being in the interest of protecting investors, will promote efficiency, competition, and capital formation; and
evaluate whether a regulation is inconsistent, incompatible, or duplicative of other Federal regulations.

subparagraph.text: In addition, in making a reasoned determination under paragraph (2)(A)(iii) of the costs and benefits of a regulation, the Commission shall, to the extent that each is relevant to the particular regulation, take into consideration the impact of the regulation on—

clause.text: investor choice;
market liquidity in the securities markets;
small businesses;
competition in the marketplace; and
investor access.

subparagraph.text: Whenever the Commission issues a final regulation that is a major rule (as defined under section 804 of title 5, United States Code), it shall state, in the regulation, the following:

clause.text: The purposes and intended consequences of the regulation.

Appropriate post-implementation quantitative and qualitative metrics to measure the economic impact of the regulation and to measure the extent practicable to which the regulation has accomplished the stated purposes.
The assessment plan that will be used, consistent with the requirements of subparagraph (B).
Any unintended or negative consequences that the Commission foresees may result from the regulation.

subparagraph.header: Requirements of assessment plan and report

clause.text: For each regulation described under subparagraph (A), the Commission shall establish an assessment plan, which shall—

subclause.text: identify the costs, benefits, and intended and unintended consequences as identified in the rulemaking release of the regulation; and
specify the data to be collected, the methods for collection and analysis of the data, and a date for completion of the assessment.

clause.text: A report on each completed assessment plan described under clause (i) shall be submitted by the Chief Economist to the Commission not later than the end of the 4-year period beginning on the date the applicable regulation is issued, unless the Commission, at the request of the Chief Economist, publishes at least 90 days before the end of such period a notice in the Federal Register extending the date and providing specific reasons why an extension is necessary.
Not later than 30 days after the Commission receives an assessment plan report under clause (ii), the Commission shall publish the report in the Federal Register for public comment.

paragraph.text: In this subsection, the term regulation—

subparagraph.text: means an agency statement of general applicability and future effect that is designed to implement, interpret, or prescribe law or policy or to describe the procedure or practice requirements of an agency, including rules, orders of general applicability, interpretive releases, and other statements of general applicability that the agency intends to have the force and effect of law; and
does not include—

clause.text: a regulation issued in accordance with the formal rulemaking provisions of section 556 or 557 of title 5, United States Code;
a regulation that is limited to agency organization, management, or personnel matters;
a regulation promulgated pursuant to statutory authority that expressly prohibits compliance with this provision; and
a regulation that is certified by the agency to be an emergency action, if such certification is published in the Federal Register.

section.header: Semiannual testimony to Congress regarding activities of the Securities and Exchange Commission
section.text: Section 4 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78d) is amended by adding at the end the following:

subsection.text: The Chairman of the Commission shall, not less than once every 6 months after the date of the enactment of this subsection, testify before the Committee on Financial Services of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs of the Senate on the activities of the Commission. At least once annually, the Commissioners shall join the Chairman with respect to testifying pursuant to the preceding sentence.

section.header: GAO audit of information technology infrastructure and handling of data
section.text: The Comptroller General of the United States shall, not later than 1 year after the date of the enactment of this Act—

paragraph.text: perform an independent audit of the information technology (IT) infrastructure of the Securities and Exchange Commission and the Commission’s handling of data, including—

subparagraph.text: a comparison of the Commission’s IT spending to other Federal financial regulators, including—

clause.text: the total amount spent on IT equipment and services; and
the amount of IT spending in proportion to each regulator’s total spending;

subparagraph.text: examining the quality and effectiveness of the Commission’s IT contracting;
determining if the Commission’s data and cybersecurity systems and procedures are sufficient; and
examining any recent Commission IT or data events, such as breaches or hacks, that may have compromised the Commission’s IT infrastructure or exposed a vulnerability; and

paragraph.text: provide to the Commission, the Committee on Financial Services of the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs of the Senate a report containing—

subparagraph.text: all findings and determinations made in conducting the audit; and
recommendations for steps that can be taken to improve the Commission’s IT infrastructure.

section.header: Consideration of cumulative effect of regulations required

subsection.header: Rules under the Securities Act of 1933
subsection.text: Section 2(b) of the Securities Act of 1933 (15 U.S.C. 77b(b)) is amended by inserting , when considered individually or cumulatively with other related rules or regulations or other related and recent proposed rules or regulations, before will promote.

subsection.header: Rules under the Securities Exchange Act of 1934
subsection.text: Section 23(a)(2) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78w(a)(2)) is amended by inserting , when considered individually or cumulatively with other related rules or regulations or other related and recent proposed rules or regulations, after which would.

subsection.header: Rules under the Investment Company Act of 1940
subsection.text: Section 2(c) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80a–2(c)) is amended by inserting , when considered individually or cumulatively with other related rules or regulations or other related and recent proposed rules or regulations, before will promote.

subsection.header: Rules under the Investment Advisers Act of 1940
subsection.text: Section 202(c) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80b–2(c)) is amended by inserting , when considered individually or cumulatively with other related rules or regulations or other related and recent proposed rules or regulations, before will promote.

section.header: Transfer of Public Company Accounting Oversight Board to Securities and Exchange Commission

subsection.text: Except as otherwise provided under this title, title I of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7211 et seq.) is amended—

paragraph.text: in the title heading, by striking Public Company Accounting Oversight Board and inserting Office of Public Accounting Oversight;
by striking the Board each place it appears and inserting the Office;
by striking The Board each place it appears and inserting The Office; and
by striking the Board’s each place it appears and inserting the Office’s.

subsection.text: Sections 104, 105, and 107 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7214; 15 U.S.C. 7215; 15 U.S.C. 7217) are repealed.

Beginning on the date that is 2 years after the date of the enactment of this Act, any reference to the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board in any law, regulation, map, document, record, or other paper of the United States shall be deemed to be a reference to the Office of Public Accounting Oversight of the Office of the Chief Accountant of the Securities and Exchange Commission.
The Public Company Accounting Oversight Board shall terminate on the date that is 2 years after the date of the enactment of this Act.
Section 101 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7211) is amended—

paragraph.text: by amending subsection (a) to read as follows:

subsection.text: There is established in the Office of the Chief Accountant of the Commission an Office of Public Accounting Oversight, to oversee the audit of companies that are subject to the securities laws, and related matters, in order to protect the interests of investors and further the public interest in the preparation of informative, accurate, and independent audit reports.

paragraph.text: by amending subsection (b) to read as follows:

subsection.text: The Chief Accountant of the Office of the Chief Accountant of the Commission shall serve as the Director of the Office of Public Accounting Oversight.

paragraph.text: in subsection (c)—

subparagraph.text: in the heading, by striking the Board and inserting the Office;
by striking , subject to action by the Commission under section 107, and once a determination is made by the Commission under subsection (d) of this section;
in paragraph (3), by striking section 104 and inserting subsection (e);
in paragraph (4), by striking section 105 and inserting subsection (f);
in paragraph (5)—

clause.text: by striking the Board (or the Commission, by rule or order) and inserting the Commission; and
by inserting and after the semicolon;

subparagraph.text: in paragraph (6)—

clause.text: by striking the rules of the Board and inserting the rules of the Commission; and
by striking ; and and inserting a period; and

subparagraph.text: by striking paragraph (7);

paragraph.text: in subsection (d)—

subparagraph.text: by striking The members of the Board and inserting The Chief Accountant of the Commission;
by striking 270 days after the date of enactment of this Act and inserting 1 year after the date of the enactment of the Streamlining Public Company Accounting Oversight Act; and
by striking the last sentence;

paragraph.text: by striking subsections (e), (f), and (g);
by inserting after subsection (d) the following:

subsection.header: Inspections of registered public accounting firms
subsection.text: The Office shall conduct a continuing program of inspections to assess the degree of compliance of each registered public accounting firm and associated persons of that firm with this Act, the rules of the Commission, or professional standards, in connection with its performance of audits, issuance of audit reports, and related matters involving issuers.
The Commission shall establish, by rule, fair procedures for the investigation and disciplining of registered public accounting firms and associated persons of such firms.

paragraph.text: by striking subsection (h).

subsection.text: The Director of the Office of Public Accounting Oversight shall, promptly after the creation of the Office, cause to be published in the Federal Register all rules that are transferred to the jurisdiction of the Office pursuant to section 2 upon the termination of the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board.

section.text: Section 102 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7212) is amended—

paragraph.text: in the section heading by striking the Board and inserting the Office;
in subsection (b)(2)(H), by striking the Board or; and
in subsection (c)(2), by striking for purposes of sections 105(d) and 107(c).

section.header: Auditing, quality control, standards, and rules
section.text: Section 103 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7213) is amended—

paragraph.text: in the heading, by striking and independence standards and inserting standards,;
in subsection (a)(3)—

subparagraph.text: in subparagraph (A)(i), by striking , subject to the terms of section 107,;
by striking subparagraph (B); and
by redesignating subparagraph (C) as subparagraph (B);

paragraph.text: in subsection (c) in the heading of paragraph (2), by striking Board and inserting Office; and
in subsection (d), by striking 101(h) and inserting 101(g).

section.text: Section 106 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7216) is amended—

paragraph.text: in the heading of subsection (a)(2), by striking Board and inserting Office; and
in subsection (c)—

subparagraph.text: by striking and the Board, subject to the approval of the Commission, may,; and
by striking (or Board).

section.text: Section 109 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7219) is amended—

paragraph.text: by amending subsection (b) to read as follows:

The standard setting body referred to in subsection (a) shall establish a budget for each fiscal year, which shall be reviewed and approved according to their respective internal procedures not less than 1 month prior to the commencement of the fiscal year to which the budget pertains.
The Commission shall establish the budget for the Office for each fiscal year.
in subsection (c)—

subparagraph.text: by amending paragraph (1) to read as follows:

The budget of the standard setting body referred to in subsection (a) for each fiscal year shall be payable from annual accounting support fees, in accordance with subsections (d) and (e). Accounting support fees and other receipts of such standard-setting body shall not be considered public monies of the United States.

The budget of the Office (reduced by any registration or annual fees received under section 102(f) for the year preceding the year for which the budget is being computed) for each fiscal year may be payable from annual accounting support fees, in accordance with subsections (d) and (e). Accounting support fees and other receipts of the Office.
in paragraph (2), by striking shall and inserting may;

paragraph.text: in subsection (d)—

subparagraph.text: in the heading, by striking the Board and inserting the Office;
in paragraph (1), by striking The Board shall establish, with the approval of the Commission, and inserting The Commission may establish;
in paragraph (2), by striking shall and inserting may; and
by striking paragraph (3);

paragraph.text: in subsection (j)—

subparagraph.text: by striking either the Board,; and
by striking , or both,; and

paragraph.text: by striking subsection (k).

section.text: Section 110 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7220) is amended—

paragraph.text: by redesignating paragraphs (5) and (6) as paragraphs (6) and (7); and
by inserting after paragraph (4) the following:
The term Office means the Office of Public Accounting Oversight of the Office of the Chief Accountant of the Commission.

subsection.text: Section 2(a)(9)(C) of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7201) is amended by striking , 101(c), 105, and 107(c) and the rules of the Board and Commission issued thereunder and inserting and 101(c) and the rules of the Commission thereunder.
Section 201(b) of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7231(b)) is amended—

paragraph.text: by striking The Board may and inserting The Commission may; and
by striking in the same manner as for rules of the Board under section 107.

subsection.text: Section 603(y)(3) of the Consumer Credit Protection Act (15 U.S.C. 1681a(y)(3)) is amended by striking any entity established under title I of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002,.
The table of contents in section 1(b) of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 is amended—

paragraph.enum: (1) by striking the item relating to title I and inserting the following new item:

toc.toc-entry: TITLE I—OFFICE OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTING OVERSIGHT

paragraph.text: in the item relating to section 102, by striking the Board and inserting the Office;
in the item relating to section 103, by striking and independence standards and inserting standards,; and
by striking the items relating to sections 104, 105, and 107.

section.header: Rule of construction with respect to cooperative arrangements
section.text: Nothing in this title, or the amendments made by this title, shall be construed to invalidate or otherwise affect a cooperative arrangement between the Public Company Accounting Oversight Board and a foreign auditor oversight authority (as defined in section 2(a) of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 (15 U.S.C. 7201(a))) in effect on the date that is 2 years after the date of the enactment of this Act.
The Securities and Exchange Commission may issue such regulations as may be necessary to carry out this title.
The amendments made by this title shall take effect on the date that is 2 years after the date of the enactment of this Act.

title.header: Study Regarding Major Rules Issued by the Securities and Exchange Commission

section.text: Section 4 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78d), as amended by section 401, is further amended by adding at the end the following:

subparagraph.text: Subject to subparagraph (C), not later than 1 year after the date of the enactment of this subsection, and every 3 years thereafter, the Comptroller General of the United States shall carry out a study on each of the major rules issued by the Commission since the last such review.
The study required under subparagraph (A) shall include, with respect to each major rule described in such subparagraph—

clause.text: a cost benefit analysis of such major rule;
a comparison between the cost benefit analysis under clause (i) and the cost benefit analysis for the same major rule carried out by the Commission;
a comparison between the projected costs of the major rule and the actual costs of the major rule; and
an evaluation of whether each major rule—

subparagraph.text: If a study required under subparagraph (A) would apply to more than 10 major rules, the Comptroller General shall review only the 10 major rules that are the most significant, as determined by the Comptroller General.

paragraph.text: Not later than 1 year after completing a study under paragraph (1), the Comptroller General shall submit to the Committee on Financial Services of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs of the Senate a report that includes—

subparagraph.text: the results of such study; and

with respect to any major rule reviewed in such study that has not yet been fully implemented by the Commission, a statement that the Comptroller General is unable to fully analyze the costs of the major rule at the time the report is submitted.

paragraph.text: In this subsection, the term major rule has the meaning given the term in section 804 of title 5, United States Code.

section.text: Section 4 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 (15 U.S.C. 78d), as amended by section 601, is further amended by adding at the end the following:

paragraph.text: With respect to a proposed rulemaking for which a public comment period is required under section 553 of title 5, United States Code, the Commission shall provide a public comment period of—

subparagraph.text: at least 60 days; or
if the Commission determines the proposed rule addresses imminent investor harm, at least 30 days.

paragraph.text: For purposes of calculating the number of days in a period under paragraph (1) with respect to a proposed rulemaking—

subparagraph.text: a Federal holiday shall not be counted; and
the period shall begin on the date the proposed rule is published in the Federal Register.

section.header: Determination of the number of violations

subsection.text: The Securities Act of 1933 is amended—

paragraph.text: in section 8A(g) (15 U.S.C. 77h–1(g)), by adding at the end the following:
For purposes of determining the number of violations for which to impose penalties under paragraph (1), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation when the acts are the result of—

subparagraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

paragraph.text: in section 20(d) (15 U.S.C. 77t(d)), by adding at the end the following:
For purposes of determining the number of violations for which to impose penalties under paragraph (1), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation when the acts are the result of—

subparagraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

subsection.text: The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 is amended—

paragraph.text: in section 21(d)(3) (15 U.S.C. 78u(d)(3)), by adding at the end the following:

subparagraph.text: For purposes of determining the number of violations for which to impose penalties under subparagraph (A)(i), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation when the acts are the result of—

clause.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

paragraph.text: in section 21B(a) (15 U.S.C. 78u–2(b)), by adding at the end the following:

paragraph.header: Determination of number of violations, acts, or omissions
paragraph.text: For purposes of determining the number of violations, acts, or omissions for which to impose penalties under this subsection, separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation, act, or omission when the acts are the result of—

subparagraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

paragraph.text: in section 32 (15 U.S.C. 78ff), by adding at the end the following:

subsection.text: For purposes of determining the number of violations for which to impose penalties under subsection (c), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation when the acts are the result of—

paragraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

subsection.text: The Investment Company Act of 1940 is amended—

paragraph.text: in section 9(d) (15 U.S.C. 80a–9(d)), by adding at the end the following:

paragraph.header: Determination of number of violations, acts, or omissions
paragraph.text: For purposes of determining the number of violations, acts, or omissions for which to impose penalties under paragraph (1), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation, act, or omission when the acts are the result of—

subparagraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

paragraph.text: in section 42(e) (15 U.S.C. 80a–41(e)), by adding at the end the following:
For purposes of determining the number of violations for which to impose penalties under paragraph (1), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation when the acts are the result of—

subparagraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

subsection.text: The Investment Advisers Act of 1940 is amended—

paragraph.text: in section 203(i) (15 U.S.C. 80b–3(i)), by adding at the end the following:

paragraph.header: Determination of number of violations, acts, or omissions
paragraph.text: For purposes of determining the number of violations, acts, or omissions for which to impose penalties under paragraph (1), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation, act, or omission when the acts are the result of—

subparagraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

paragraph.text: in section 209(e) (15 U.S.C. 80b–9(e)), by adding at the end the following:
For purposes of determining the number of violations for which to impose penalties under paragraph (1), separate acts of noncompliance are a single violation when the acts are the result of—

subparagraph.text: a common or a substantially overlapping originating cause;
the same misstatement or omission; or
a continuing failure to comply.

subsection.header: Review of the organization of the Commission

paragraph.text: Not later than 180 days after the date of enactment of this Act, the Chairman of the Securities and Exchange Commission shall conduct a review of the organizational structure of the Commission, including the offices and officials that report directly to the Chairman.

Not later than 90 days after completing the review required under paragraph (1), the Chairman shall, to the extent practicable and consistent with applicable law, reduce the number of offices and officials reporting directly to the Chairman by reassigning such offices and officials within the organizational structure of the Commission.

Not later than 30 days after completing the reorganization required under paragraph (2), the Chairman shall submit to the Committee on Financial Services of the House of Representatives and the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs of the Senate a report containing—

subparagraph.text: a description of the offices and officials that reported directly to the Chairman on the date of enactment of this Act;
an identification of the offices and officials whose reporting relationships are required by statute;
a description of the changes made pursuant to paragraph (2);
an identification of any offices or officials that continue to report directly to the Chairman and the reasons such reporting relationships were retained; and
any legislative recommendations for further reducing the number of offices and officials reporting directly to the Chairman.

paragraph.text: This subsection shall not prohibit the Commission from reorganizing the offices described in this subsection in the future, if the Commission determines such reorganization is necessary or appropriate in the public interest or for the protection of investors.

subsection.text: The Securities and Exchange Commission shall, if the Commission determines it appropriate, consolidate the regional offices of the Commission.
